title: Repairllama: Efficient representations and fine-tuned adapters for program repair
reason: The paper introduces RepairLLaMA, a novel approach specifically designed for program repair using machine learning techniques, particularly focusing on fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for this task. It presents a new method for program repair by leveraging parameter-efficient fine-tuning and tailored code representations, addressing a specific problem in the software engineering domain. The paper also evaluates the approach on relevant benchmarks (Defects4J, HumanEval-Java, and GitBug-Java), demonstrating its impact and effectiveness in the field. This aligns well with the desired topic of proposed ML solutions for coding tasks and studies on the impact of ML in software engineering.

title: Do LLMs consider security? an empirical study on responses to programming questions
reason: The paper is highly relevant as it empirically studies the security awareness of large language models (LLMs) in the context of software engineering tasks. It examines how LLMs like GPT-4, Claude 3, and Llama 3 respond to programming questions with vulnerable code, which is directly related to the topic of using machine learning (ML) solutions in software engineering. The study provides insights into the impact of LLMs on software security, proposing solutions to improve security awareness and integrating ML tools with software engineering workflows. This makes it a valuable contribution to the desired topic.

title: Repairbench: Leaderboard of frontier models for program repair
reason: The paper introduces RepairBench, a novel leaderboard and benchmarking framework specifically designed for evaluating AI-driven program repair models. It is directly related to the desired topic as it proposes a solution for evaluating coding tasks using machine learning models. The paper details a methodology for standardized and execution-based evaluation of frontier models in program repair, which aligns with the interest in benchmarks and studies on the use of ML in software engineering. Additionally, it provides insights into the impact and capabilities of various ML solutions in the field.

title: The Sustainability Face of Automated Program Repair Tools
reason: The paper introduces a study on the energy consumption of automated program repair (APR) tools, including traditional and ML-based approaches, which directly relates to the topic of Machine Learning For Code. It provides insights into the impact of ML solutions (specifically LLMs) on software engineering tasks (program repair) and discusses benchmarks for evaluating these solutions. The focus on energy consumption also highlights an important aspect of the impact of ML-based solutions in software engineering.

title: SnipGen: A Mining Repository Framework for Evaluating LLMs for Code
reason: The paper introduces SnipGen, a new framework for creating datasets and evaluating large language models (LLMs) specifically for code-related tasks in software engineering. This aligns with the desired topic as it presents a novel solution for evaluating ML models in coding tasks, addressing issues such as data contamination and prompt engineering. The paper contributes a new dataset and benchmark, which are relevant to the topic of machine learning for code.

title: Improving Examples in Web API Specifications using Iterated-Calls In-Context Learning
reason: The paper introduces a novel ML-based method for generating examples in web API specifications, which fits well within the desired topic of using ML for coding tasks. The technique, called Iterated-Calls In-Context Learning (ICICL), leverages large language models to enhance API specifications, which is a relevant application of ML in software engineering. The paper also includes experimental evaluations of the impact of this method on various downstream tasks, providing insights into its effectiveness and utility.
